text: The surgeon injects vasopressin into the tissue surrounding a large intramural myoma, using a dilution of five international units in twenty milliliters of saline.
The uterus exhibits a marble white appearance due to the vasopressin injection, and the serosa over the myoma is visibly starting to bulge.
Approximately 100 ml of saline is injected into the surgical site to achieve hemostasis and facilitate hydro dissection during the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon creates ischemia in the myoma, resulting in a marble white appearance of the tissue, indicating reduced blood flow. This transient ischemia is intended to last for twenty minutes to facilitate the surgical removal of the myoma.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an anterior longitudinal incision over the serosal surface and uterine muscles, providing access to the intramural myoma.
The surgeon continues the incision through all layers of the uterine muscle to reach the intramural myoma, ensuring access to the fibroid for removal.
The surgeon observes the pearly white color appearance indicating the visibility of the myoma capsule, which is a sign that the capsule has started to become visible.
As we know harmonic is a friction energy and that advantage is that once it vibrates it breaks the hydrogen bond and tissue is created.
The surgeon applies a myoma screw from the opposite side to provide anterior traction, facilitating the enucleation of the myoma.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigation instrument to articulate and separate the myoma from the surrounding muscle layers of the uterus.
The surgeon uses a second myoma screw from the opposite side to provide better articulation and facilitate the enucleation of the intramural myoma.
The surgeon uses a single myoma screw to apply traction on the myoma during the four-port laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon utilizes two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port, positioned according to the baseball diamond concept, to optimize the manipulation angle during the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon rearranges the myoma screw to continue enucleating the myoma, with approximately half of the myoma visibly extracted from the uterine muscle layers. The remaining portion is carefully separated without excessive avulsion force.
The surgeon positions the myoma screw on the left side and uses both hands to enucleate the myoma from the surrounding uterine tissue.

The surgeon discovers a lobulated myoma, initially appearing as a single mass, with a larger continuation behind it. Careful enucleation is performed to ensure complete removal of both portions without leaving any tissue behind.
The surgeon successfully removes the entire myoma from the uterine muscle without opening the cavity, completing the enucleation process.
The surgeon begins suturing the area where the myoma was removed, with the surgical field appearing completely dry and free of bleeding, indicating no damage to feeding vessels.
The surgeon begins applying sutures to obliterate the dead space, using an extracorporeal knot technique to secure the first layer.
The surgeon uses a 30mm endoske needle and number one Vicryl suture to perform continuous suturing with a dandy jamming knot technique, ensuring proper closure of the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a darker knot pusher to achieve a dumbling effect, effectively obliterating dead space. Misra's knot is applied with an additional lock to convert the slip knot into a secure lock knot, preventing it from slipping back.
The surgeon cuts the suture to a longer length, preparing to trim it later, with the surgical field appearing dry and free of bleeding.
The surgeon applies tension sutures to approximate the tissue layers, ensuring they are securely overlocked.
The surgeon demonstrates the application of Misra's knot, showing multiple hitches, winds, and locks, followed by an extra lock to secure the approximation of the uterine wall.

The surgeon demonstrates the knot technique, showing one hitch, one wind, and one lock, followed by a second wind and lock, and a third wind and lock. The knot is then pushed using the Vandarkar knot pusher.
The surgeon observes the effect of vasopressin wearing off, prompting a rapid closure of the knots to prevent bleeding from starting.
The surgeon slides the western knot using the medline, demonstrating an extracorporeal knot technique, and pulls the tail to securely lock the knot in place.

The surgeon uses multiple knots to approximate the dead space, demonstrating the Dandy jamming knot technique with continuous suturing. The suture is pulled through a loop, automatically locking it in place.
The surgeon slides the knot to lock it in place and begins continuous suturing to approximate the edges and close the serous layer.

An assistant holds the uterus while the surgeon takes bites and locks the sutures, visibly flattening the uterus. The flattened appearance is due to the power of the knot, and the uterus is expected to regain its normal shape after healing.
The surgeon continues to lock the sutures, ensuring that all spaces between the tissue layers are closed.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon performs continuous suturing, taking deep bites into the uterine layers. An assistant holds the suture to maintain tension and ensure the margins are adequately aligned.
The surgeon uses the tail of the previous extracorporeal knot to lock and terminate the continuous suturing, ensuring the closure of the surgical site.

The surgeon ties two to three knots to secure the suture, followed by thorough irrigation and suction to check for bleeding. The posterior cul-de-sac is cleared of any collected blood, confirming the absence of active bleeding.

The surgeon lifts the patient's head during suctioning, revealing the myoma placed in the right iliac fossa. The uterus appears clean, and the pelvic cavity is washed out as the fluid used during the procedure is suctioned out, leaving some fluid to prevent adhesions.

The surgeon pulls the myoma from the anterior and posterior cul-de-sac, preparing for morselation. The morselation process begins, gradually reducing the size of the myoma through continuous mechanical fragmentation.

Just to decrease the size of this video we are not showing you the entire morselation because it is time taking but the entire tissue is morselated and it was pulled out. Thank you very much for watching this video.
